speaker: interviewer
text: And we also-- she also left out--

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you just going back it out one angle?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. [INAUDIBLE] ?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. We'll start-- I'll ask you about what Joli told you-- what Yoli told you if-- when they were asking you questions, what-- what she suggested that you tell them.

speaker: interviewee
text: She told me--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, wait a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and camera is rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two. This is Sarah Salamon.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one time--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you continue, will you tell us what Yoli told you, if they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yoli told me every night when I came from the-- from the SS office, she says, Sarah, she says, they started to beat me. And the kindness went away. And I don't know if I can exist like this. She says, listen, you are dead anyhow. You have nothing to lose. You have a lot to gain. She was a nice couple years older from me. And she was very educated.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the night, I thought, what should I-- I-- I have to hold myself? I came home every evening, blue, black, my body was. And one day, one brutal SS man-- I will never forget his face-- he give it to me. Oh, my God, if I holded out-- and I thank that my mother was with me-- What was he trying to get you to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: That this is the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not a shower?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a shower. And I says, I saw a shower.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you say that, instead of a crematorium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? Because Yoli told me this to say. Hold you the-- for the first word, what you say is, this, hold you to the last. Or you will come out and come back to my-- or me, or they will kill you. You have an-- you have not an-- you have not a choice. And I was young, healthy. And I holded out, but I came back the couple days. She went there where I met my father's cousin. She was a doctor too on the [GERMAN]. A [GERMAN] is German hospital. And she says, Sasha, when you will be-- feel better, I take you, you will work with me. You have nursing qualification, and you will help me, because I can't work day and night. She was-- I think she's in New York now. Her husband died.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name? Bimko. This is her name. This is her-- close. Mine father's, or brother's, or the-- or something like.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she recognized me. She took me every day from Yoli's block, and I should give care of the sick people. And this way, I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what-- after the-- the SS stopped questioning you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --then you went to work for your cousin, Bimko?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, after this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there appels in between? Or were there appels--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the [GERMAN], not. You didn't got much room to make appel. To sick people can't go out. They was half dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, but when you're with Yoli, were there appels?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. But she didn't let me go out to the appel. I was very skinny, you know? And especially, I wasn't to eat enough. So she says, stay in the block. Stay in the-- Ilka, you will give care.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed with Ilka and--

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in the block, yeah. And then, when was some office work, you know, I'm a good typer, especially in German and in Polish, and she give me the work. What she used to do, then, I did it. And she used to get, from the kitchen, a little bit more food, you know? And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Yoli's position?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was the block [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a kapo?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, a kapo was off the street, but she was inside. She gave the food the morning, food, a little-- a little bit [NON-ENGLISH]. And she gived out the breakfast, and the lunch, and what the comes-- comed in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and your work, when you were with Yoli was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- we was-- me and Ilka, we used to watch the-- you know, one was always in the room when was [GERMAN], you know? Then, she says, (QUIETLY) under the bed! Under the bed! To hide it. Because of her, then a lot of people survived from this block. Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in Auschwitz for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, was long enough, my dear. And then, when the Russian came closer to Auschwitz, then they-- they took us out again, you know, in-- in open boxes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In trucks?

speaker: interviewee
text: In trucks. And our food was the snow would fall on-- in-- in the-- in the mouth. But every time was easier, because even the soldiers run away. They took us in a train, a empty train. And no food didn't come from nowhere. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who was watching you at this time? Who was guarding you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wehrmacht. This is soldiers what not SS, just the Wehrmacht. And once, came a soldier. And-- and we talked Polish. Then, he started to answer us Polish. He was from the Volksdeutsche, you know? And once, in the evening, he was hungry too. They didn't got what to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: The guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He says, could I have a couple girls healthy? We will go in the evening. There is a-- a colony, farmers, and we will ask they should give us something to eat. He went in the evening, four girls-- four girls and two guards. And they went-- we came to a farmer and knocked in the door in the middle of the night. So you went with them? Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they choose you, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there was-- the soldiers told us, this is from Poland, the Volksdeutsche. And they speak Polish. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did the German speak Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: German?

speaker: interviewer
text: The Volksdeutsche?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they are-- they are from the-- from Poland, born in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the guards, did they speak Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They are from the Wehrmacht. It's called the Wehrmacht.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we came-- I knocked in the door. And she come in, a man. What you child wanted? I says, we wanted some food. We staying here in the [NON-ENGLISH], it's called. We don't have-- we didn't eat already, I-- I don't know how long. And some-- some girls started to cry. The-- the-- the lady took us in, and prepared something to eat, and give us clothes. With the bare foot we went, in wintertime. And the man, head husband, says, Polish. Look what you have from clothes and give it for the kids. Well, we was all in this age, you know? And she give us shoes and-- and knitted stockings, warm, panties, old coats, what they didn't use it. And for-- for everything, a little bit. And the woman says, should we give the soldiers too? So I-- I says, they are from the Volksdeutsche too. They give flour and-- it's not rice, it's-- it's like [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And they give us a couple pots, we should have-- make a fire, and potatoes, meat, kielbasa, wurst. And we got all so loaded, and brought back. Then, we started there to cook. How many were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were-- we was a lot, but a lot died, you know, in-- in the cold and the-- then-- then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what year this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was, I think, a nice couple months before the [NON-ENGLISH], you know? Before--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the English troops came into Bergen-Belsen. We-- we walked 100 miles to Bergen-Belsen. Everyone got the feet frozen. And then, we in-- [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Before-- before you went to Bergen-Belsen, you-- you were-- you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Mauthausen. And then, I was in-- just a moment. I-- from Auschwitz, we went to-- oh, how it's called? [THINKING] This will come in my mind. Mauthausen, and then was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there, I met the first time there, Mengele. Mengele, you know, from Auschwitz. And he changed. He--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you-- you say you met with Mengele.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in Auschwitz. Did you get--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz, I saw him every time. He make [GERMAN]. It's-- this is-- the finger went. He didn't talk. So and so. Right was to live, and so is the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you go a tattoo when you were in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: A-- a-- a number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in Auschwitz, I was. This is from-- from the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what it was like getting the tattoo?

speaker: interviewee
text: What should I describe? If you take a-- a-- a knife and pick in the flesh, and the blood was running What is to describe? Simple things.

speaker: interviewer
text: After they took you out of Auschwitz and you went with the Wehrmacht by-- by truck, they-- they took you to where? Was it to Gross-Rosen then? Because you--

speaker: interviewee
text: First, to Gross-Rosen, and then to Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Gross-Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Gross-Rosen, we might been in not-- Mengele operated the-- you know, they cut up the fingers, and some the feet, because there was gangrene, all-- all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the toes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Infected, yeah. And he was sweet, like sugar. And I look at him. He says, [GERMAN]? Why you look at me? I says, I don't recognize you, Dr. Mengele.

speaker: interviewer
text: You worked with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you were a nurse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because I help-- I give-- I make the dressing. He cut, and I made the dressing, and-- and another couple nurses. And I said-- I-- I-- and he says, children, you-- we should help everyone, because there will soon be a finish. And I looked at him, and I says, oh, my God, how a person can change. It's a pleasure to look at you, Dr. Mengele. You know, I never saw such a gorgeous man.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean, good looking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mengele, yeah. Like a angel, so good looking. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SMILING] I don't-- I-- I-- I couldn't understand how a person can change himself like this. Please, kids, and, let's do what more-- what more that we can save the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think there was this change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they know that they are down already.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was near the end of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a couple months, maybe was. This was in the middle winter, like now. And-- but-- what is next month here?

speaker: interviewer
text: February.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like in February, yeah. Then, we came to Bergen-Belsen. And 15th of April, we was free.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with Yoli when you were in Gross-Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every time, with Yoli?

speaker: interviewer
text: And from Gross-Rosen, you went to where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us, how did you get from Gross-Rosen to Mauthausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: How? Walking, just walking, weeks. We-- we-- we went for a rest in a pig-- where they raise pigs. Then, I steal the food from-- from the pigs. We went, a couple girls, and steal the food. And this what we ate a couple days. Not much, a little bit. Yoli was the-- she give a-- a little bit, and just have something in the body.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there snow on the ground?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too much. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeah. When we passed German cities-- small, you know-- then the-- the German families threw bread, and clothes, blankets of us. And the SS shoot them, shoot through the windows, because they shouldn't do this. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [SHAKES HEAD] You know, you have to have [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS] too much pens and paper to describe what a person can feel and can do it. Sometimes, [EMOTIONAL] I can't sleep, because it comes always. Then I think, my God how I did that? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is un-possible-- no soap, not a-- a clean towel.

speaker: interviewee
text: I broke-- a SS woman break my leg with a iron stick. And I didn't got nothing to make a band-aid, to put this-- I knew it from mine nursing qualification. Then, I took a dirty shirt off from me and make band-aid and put together. And this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: She give me with that stick, because I couldn't walk quick, like she was-- she wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this? Where did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz, still. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, I survived. Then, when-- when they started the talk in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mauthausen-- you were-- you were telling me you were walking to Mauthausen. Can you tell me--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- no. We walked, and we was-- every minute, you heard in the-- in-- in-- in the back-- [MAKES GUN SHOT NOISES]. They shot the people because, if they can't walk, what can a soldier do there? He would not take it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were only women?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Women and men, together. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And at Mauthausen, you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Mauthausen like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Awful. No food. They give a couple coats. Then, one thing what was, they sew in. And we founded there diamonds, in the-- in the clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the lining?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the lining, yeah. In the-- [MOVES HAND ON ARM].

speaker: interviewer
text: In the sleeves?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the sleeves. And I don't know from where the transport came there, but there must be a very rich people-- diamonds, American gold, money. I opened it once, a [NON-ENGLISH]. Then, there started to-- like beads fell out.

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, what-- I says, Yoli, what we will do with this? If they catch us, they kill us all. She says, don't worry. There is French men working, private. And they will-- they will sell us food for this. And this while like this. We went into a city and where the French men was working. And they ask us, have you some gold? Some diamond jewelry? And Yoli says, yes, I have some. What would you like for this? She says bread, bread, or something to eat. We are three sisters here, and we would like survive. And it changed, you know? Then, we went to work there, night shift. The-- the French men, were they just-- did they-- were they free individuals? Or were they also prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: They wasn't free, but not prisoners. And they became food from the house-- from the home. Their families sent. Or maybe Red Cross sent them. But they sent them very nice food-- salami, and meat roasted, and bread, white bread. This what keep us a little bit, you know? And we-- they took us for a couple weeks, because we wasn't long there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the people who belonged to these clothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: [CHUCKLES] They went straight to the crematorium. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Straight. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing while you were in Mauthausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we was sitting-- they was told something, then we repair. They give us a little bit thread and needle. And not-- not long, we was. I think about three weeks, or four weeks, that this was all. Because the front came always near. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Yeah. And-- and they brought food. Mine God, it's like the-- everything looked like black coffee. But-- and hunger is not, I will not eat this. We eat this. And who didn't-- who didn't-- can't eat, didn't survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do other work while in-- while you were at Mauthausen, other then sewing? Or did you see other things going on in-- in Mauthausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Was a big crematorium. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] they schlepped the people what's dead, [INAUDIBLE], like, you know, dogs. They wanted bread? Put this in the crematorium. They should-- they was on the street and every place-- dead people. And you know what? That a human being is just like a animal. Sometimes, when we go out in the night, you know, to the bathroom or so, and there wasn't a dead person is, oh, they didn't put any here. We have to go in-- in-- in the-- in-- in-- in the dirt. And you know, so selfish you was, you fight for your life. This is un-possible that, normal, a person can do it this. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But sometimes, in Auschwitz, there was a lot of [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] soldiers from the Wehrmacht. You know, they used to bring bread and a salami, and they threw on the-- on-- on-- on-- on the [NON-ENGLISH] where we sleeped. And they just say, share with another one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever get any parcels from anybody when you were in-- in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: One parcel, I got from this friend from my mother, a Polish man, Jwardzik. He got three daughters. And we-- every summer, I used the come to them on the farm, my mother and me, and my, sometimes, father came in. One parcel, I got.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he-- was he Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, a-- a Catholic. And he didn't send of my name, because I could write a letter. And he sent of Ilka's name. And she got-- she was from Czechoslovakia. And she-- and-- and she received-- received the package. Then, he sent dry bread-- they make it, like toast, in the oven-- and baked meat, slices, the fat one, onion, garlic-- this, I remember-- and some other things. And Yoli says, Sasha, I got a parcel today, but this is yours.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you shared it with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue after-- next tape.
